content.thinking:
The NetworkPolicy is restricting ingress to only port 8080 (HTTP). This means port 26257 (SQL/gRPC) ingress is blocked from external clients. However, the pods themselves need to communicate with each other on port 26257. Let me check if inter-pod communication is blocked or if this only affects external access.

Looking at the NetworkPolicy spec:
- It only has one ingress rule: allow port 8080
- policyTypes: ["Ingress"] - this means it controls ingress only
- Since there's only one ingress rule (port 8080), all other ingress (including port 26257) is implicitly blocked
- This would block inter-pod SQL/gRPC communication between the CockroachDB pods, which could cause issues during rolling updates. But the cluster is currently healthy, suggesting either the pods are already connected with persistent connections, or I'm missing something about how the NetworkPolicy is being applied.

Actually, the NetworkPolicy blocks ingress on all ports except 8080. When a pod restarts during the upgrade, it needs to re-establish connections on port 26257 to rejoin the cluster - and this NetworkPolicy would block that. The task hints that I should work around NetworkPolicy issues rather than assume the cluster itself is unhealthy. I should modify the NetworkPolicy to allow port 26257 for inter-pod communication during the upgrade. When the pods restart during the rolling upgrade, they'll lose their existing connections and need to establish new ones on port 26257, which the current NetworkPolicy blocks. I need to update the policy to allow inter-pod communication on that port so the cluster can reform after each node restarts.

For the upgrade itself, since this is a minor patch version bump, I can use a straightforward rolling update strategy rather than manually managing the partition. The StatefulSet will update pods one at a time, waiting for each to be ready before moving to the next. I'll go with the partition-based approach for a controlled upgrade—first fixing the NetworkPolicy to allow inter-pod communication on port 26257, then patching the image while managing the partition to roll out one pod at a time.

content.text: The NetworkPolicy only allows ingress on port 8080, blocking port 26257 (SQL/gRPC). Restarted pods during the upgrade won't be able to rejoin the cluster. I'll fix the NetworkPolicy first, then do the controlled rolling upgrade.

input.description: Patch NetworkPolicy to allow inter-pod communication on port 26257

content.text: Now set partition=3 to hold automatic updates, then patch the image to v24.1.1:

input.command: kubectl -n cockroachdb patch sts crdb-cluster -p '{"spec":{"updateStrategy":{"rollingUpdate":{"partition":3}}}}' && echo "partition set to 3" && kubectl -n cockroachdb patch sts crdb-cluster -p '{"spec":{"template":{"spec":{"containers":[{"name":"cockroachdb","image":"cockroachdb/cockroach:v24.1.1"}]}}}}' && echo "image patched to v24.1.1"

input.command:
for i in $(seq 1 30); do
  STATUS=$(kubectl -n cockroachdb get pod crdb-cluster-2 -o jsonpath='{.status.phase}:{.status.containerStatuses[0].ready}:{.spec.containers[0].image}' 2>/dev/null)
  echo "[$(date +%H:%M:%S)] crdb-cluster-2: $STATUS"
  if echo "$STATUS" | grep -q "Running:true:cockroachdb/cockroach:v24.1.1"; then
    echo "crdb-cluster-2 is ready on v24.1.1"
    break
  fi
  sleep 10
done
